item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This just keeps getting more and more absurd and I am loving it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1594: HOLY SHIT MORE PEOPLE

FSS-XXI: People? How many?

(1 minute pause)

SCP-1594: TWENTY

SCP-1594: THEY'RE BEING EATEN

FSS-XXI: By the instances of SCP-1594-A?

(4 minute pause)

SCP-1594: YES

SCP-1594: WAIT HOLD ON

(2 minute pause)

SCP-1594: NOT EATEN

SCP-1594: USED AS LUBE


I can't stop laughing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1594: HUNDREDS OF NOODLES HERE
SCP-1594: THOUSANDS

SCP-1594: SLIME COATED SMELLS LIKE FUDGE

SCP-1594: NOT EATEN
SCP-1594: USED AS LUBE

SCP-1594: WORM FUCK

I'm unsure if I love this because it's creepy, intriguing, hilarious, or all three, but I know one thing for sure, that this get's my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now, I am aware this is a necropost, however, this person's question has gone unanswered for quite some time. In my opinion, it may simply be because they do not appear like worms, but are much skinnier and longer like such a noodle. I am not the author, obviously, but this is merely my interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It kind of has two seemingly unrelated phenomena with two different types of weird (ghosts affected by gravity with everything that logically follows, fudge noodles that hunt people by triggering volcanoes and use them for lube), but it's just the right amount of terrifying and insane.

Of note, why do they generally describe these things as noodles and not worms if they know what a worm is?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tentative upvote. I absolutely love it when a skip is able to imply something much more insidious or horrifying than what the document actually describes (see SCP-1193 for the best example of this), since it shows that sometimes the Foundation doesn't have the whole picture and is only able to focus on a tiny piece of the puzzle. The brevity of the soldiers' communications helped make the logs unsettling and mysterious, and stuff like "WORM FUCK" was vague enough to leave room for interpretation. I do think that the reveal that the worms were having an orgy (or something?) and using people as lube, while delightfully squicky, was a bit too absurd and over-the-top for me to take 100% seriously. But the overall strangeness of the skip and its presentation are good enough to warrant a +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The detonation momentarily exposed a previously unknown volcanic caldera, which emitted an intense odor of fudge

Wat.

Here's an upvote, dammit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"HOLY SHIT MORE PEOPLE"

"WORM FUCK"

I have no idea what's going on here, but I like it. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 WORM FUCK from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really like it. It's this nice, creative blend of bizarre and creepy that I think makes for the best SCPs. It's also one of those that REALLY gets you speculating as to what could have caused their current situation. However, the last three communications are kind of jarring.

People used as lube? Really? And the next transcript seems to confirm it's the sexual kind too. That's not really creepy, just kind of goofy.

And the final one seems off to me in the sense that i don't understand why the SCP foundaiton would ignore the communication. You'd think after three years without a sign of activity, they would reply as quickly as possible when they get one.

So yeah, if you ignore those last three transcripts, it's an excellent SCP in my book. Maybe a quick rewrite could fix the one problem, maybe take the opportunity to reveal a bit more of the soldiers' history. Still, +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't really feeling it at the end, and this sums it up for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh… I was reading it purely for its tag to get some inspiration.

Too lazy to critique, but you should read this.

http://www.scp-wiki.net/conservation-of-wtf

I know that the "SPAGHETTI NOODLE" is referring to what SCP-1594-A look like but I can't help but think that it's the flying spaghetti monster.

But no they're just 'fucking' worms. Flying spaghetti monsters would be too much anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh god, this made me terrified and nauseous at the same time. +1

EDIT: WORM FUCK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how this takes a basic idea (intangible people) and takes it to its logical conclusion (intangible people AT THE NEAR-CENTER OF THE EARTH) and makes it horrifying ("WORM FUCK").

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm somewhat intrigued by this. Take a cookie (and a +1).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconding this. The lube thing is hard to take seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1594: WE MUST BE DEAD

FSS-XI: For what reason do you believe that?

To be fair, that's a reasonable conclusion to reach in the described circumstances of being in a location incompatible with life as we know it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, eight people, then? I can work with that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sixteen people is a massive squad.

(standard) Army infantry units explained:

Team: 4 people, including the TL, usually a SGT, sometimes a CPL or SPC.

Squad: 2 teams, + squad leader.

Platoon: 4 squads + Platoon Leader + Platoon Sergeant + RTO + Doc

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It feels like there was a lot of effort put into trying to get these guys that we don't even see because they found out it was pretty much impossible to do anything with them but communicate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yep, it's a Berggren. Totally called it.

Magnificently weird. Well done. Now if you'll excuse me, I think I need to go lie down and examine my life choices.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured as much :P. Roger wilco.

E: How's that, then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mostly they were not just planning their words but watching whatever they then reacted to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WORM FUCK

Upvoted.

Nitpicks: squads have leaders, not commanders. And they generally lead squads. Not platoons. 16 people is also really small, infantry platoons in the Army are 40 people (39 grunts plus doc)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was feeling a bit meh about this until I read the communications logs and then it was just wtf. So I upvoted :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, and I up voted, but there are a few issues. For one thing, the dialogue definitely needs some reworking - people don't spend eight minutes thinking about their words, only to exclaim profanity. Furthermore, I really don't know if the cross-links are necessary - one, maybe, but two is a bit much. Still, they don't actually detract from the article, which is more than can be said most of the time.

Also, I too enjoyed the Warehouse 13 reference. I would be incredibly surprised if it wasn't intentional - after all, even in the strange world of the Foundation, the smell of fudge is just odd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wasn't sure about this one at first, but the logs ended up selling it. That said, I think the dialogue is a bit off. Especially considering that these are soldiers from the 1970's, and they have to speak together to be heard (and thereby must plan their responses), some of their phrases seemed off. Still, the horror of what these guys are experiencing was enough to put it into upvote territory for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The 'lube' thing and subsequent implications were a bit much, but this is otherwise quite good. The cross links were an especially nice surprise- for them to be both relevant and interesting is a rare thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Giant subterranean worm orgy? I can dig that. This though:

emitted an intense odor of fudge

I dunno if that was an intentional Warehouse 13 plug or not, but either way it made me chuckle. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dunno what I just read but I like it and I like articles that do this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All I can think is "What the flying fuck did I just read?"

It's well written, albeit confusing at times, and definatly strange.

Upvote for the sheer what the fuck factor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well this is… Interesting…
